sound_type: piercing whistle alarm -- a sustained, high-pitched whistle that carries across mountain valleys, the 76th acoustic element, the loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine, E=5, audible at 300-500 meters, colony-wide alarm, graded for predator type, the 'whistle pig'
ecosystem: alpine and subalpine rocky slopes -- boulder fields, talus margins, rocky meadow edges, and cliff bases at elevations of 1,200-3,500 meters. Rock-associated habitat with burrow systems dug into rocky soil. Adjacent alpine meadows provide forage.
season: active spring through fall (April/May to September/October); hibernates 7-8 months through winter

coordinates.description: Reference for rocky slope habitat in the eastern Washington Cascades. The Yellow-bellied Marmot inhabits rocky slopes and alpine meadows. The piercing whistle carries across mountain valleys on clear days.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY: Sciuridae (35th). Fourteenth mammalian entry. Twelfth mammalian species. RODENTIA RETURNS -- second rodent family after Castoridae (Beaver, 61). Same order, different family. Castoridae and Sciuridae diverged approximately 50-60 million years ago. The marmot is a rodent (single pair of continuously growing upper incisors), NOT a lagomorph.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Placentalia continues. The marmot is a placental mammal in order Rodentia.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Marmota from Latin mus montanus ('mountain mouse'), through French marmotte. flaviventris from Latin flavus ('yellow') + ventris ('belly'). Marmota flaviventris = 'yellow-bellied mountain mouse.' Common name 'whistle pig' from the piercing alarm call.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Marmota -- approximately 15 species worldwide across the Northern Hemisphere. Three in North America: M. monax (Woodchuck), M. flaviventris (Yellow-bellied), M. caligata (Hoary Marmot).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Rodentia -- RETURNS. Second Rodentia family in the engine after Castoridae (Beaver, 61). Rodentia is the largest mammalian order (~2,500 species, >40% of all mammals). Key rodent feature: single pair of continuously growing upper incisors (unlike lagomorphs with four upper incisors). Aquatic (Beaver) to alpine (marmot). River to summit.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Mammalia -- fourteenth mammalian entry. Twelfth mammalian species. FIVE orders (Rodentia 2 families, Carnivora 5 families, Didelphimorphia 1 family, Lagomorpha 2 families). NINE families. TWO infraclasses (Placentalia, Marsupialia).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Sciuridae's FIRST entry at degree 75. The 35th family in the engine. NEW FAMILY. Sixth new family in seven degrees (Mephitidae 69, Procyonidae 70, Canidae 71, Didelphidae 72, Ochotonidae 74, Sciuridae 75). Sciuridae contains ~300 species in ~50 genera -- one of the largest mammalian families.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: Approximately 2,000-6,000 Hz. The whistle is a sustained, pure-toned, high-pitched vocalization.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Approximately 3,000-5,000 Hz (the piercing core of the whistle).
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song. ALARM WHISTLE. Four primary types: (1) Single whistle -- sustained alarm (0.5-2 sec), first detection. (2) Repeated whistle series -- escalation, 2-5 calls. (3) Trill/chatter -- close-range social. (4) Scream -- extreme distress.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Single whistle: 0.5-2 seconds. Repeated series: 3-10 seconds total. Alarm calling can persist intermittently for minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single sustained whistle with slight rising pitch before trailing off. Clean onset, sustained core, gradual offset. Pure-toned rather than harmonic-rich.
song_metrics.note_types: Piercing whistle (alarm), repeated whistle series (escalation), trill/chatter (social), scream (distress).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single whistle or repeated series with 1-3 second intervals. Graded: single whistle for distant threat, repeated series for approaching threat. No complex phrase structure.
song_metrics.timbre: Clear, pure-toned, piercing -- like a referee's whistle or a kettle's steam. The whistle has a remarkably pure tone for a mammalian vocalization. No growl or rasp. Clean and cutting.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5. LOUD. The loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine. Audible at 300-500 meters across open rocky terrain. Far louder than the Pika's bark (E=4). The whistle is the primary indicator of marmot presence.
song_metrics.tempo: Single whistle: one call. Repeated series: 2-5 whistles with 1-3 second intervals. Overall tempo during alarm: moderate to rapid depending on threat level.
song_metrics.context: ALARM and SOCIAL. Whistle: colony-wide alarm when predator detected. Graded for predator type (aerial vs. terrestrial -- different call patterns trigger different responses). Trill/chatter: close-range social interactions (greeting, aggression, mating). The marmot is acoustically active during its surface activity periods (morning through late afternoon).
song_metrics.regional_variation: Some geographic variation in whistle structure across populations, but the basic piercing whistle is consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: PIERCING WHISTLE ALARM -- the 76th acoustic element. A sustained, high-pitched whistle that carries 300-500 m across mountain terrain. E=5. The loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine. Colony-wide alarm. Graded for predator type. The 'whistle pig' -- the sound IS the species.

physical_description.overall: Large, stocky ground squirrel. 470-670 mm total length including bushy tail of 130-220 mm. Weight varies dramatically: 1.6-2.5 kg at spring emergence, 3.5-5 kg at pre-hibernation peak. The largest squirrel-family member in the PNW. Robust, barrel-shaped body with short powerful limbs.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 75, the diagnostic features are: (1) YELLOW-ORANGE BELLY PATCH -- the namesake feature, visible when sitting upright. (2) GRIZZLED BROWN-GRAY DORSUM -- individual hairs banded dark and buff. (3) WHITE FRONTAL BLAZE -- white patches between the eyes. (4) BUFF CHEEKS. (5) BUSHY TAIL -- furred, 130-220 mm. (6) STOCKY BARREL SHAPE -- heavy-set, ground-hugging build. (7) STRONG CURVED CLAWS -- adapted for digging burrows.
physical_description.fur_detail: DORSUM: Grizzled brown-gray, individual guard hairs banded dark brown and buff. VENTRAL: Diagnostic yellow-orange patch. Underfur dense and soft. One molt per year (spring). Not as dense as Pika's fur -- the marmot hibernates rather than enduring winter surface activity.
physical_description.dental_detail: Rodent dentition with a single pair of continuously growing upper incisors (the key distinction from lagomorphs, which have four upper incisors). Adapted for herbivory -- grasses, forbs, wildflowers.
physical_description.field_id: On rocky slopes at mid-to-high elevation: a large, stocky, brown ground squirrel sitting upright on a prominent boulder, often basking in the sun. Yellow-orange belly visible when upright. Grizzled brown-gray back. White patches between eyes. Bushy tail. Delivers a piercing whistle alarm when disturbed. Distinguished from Hoary Marmot (larger, more silvery, higher elevation) and ground squirrels (smaller) by size, color, and the diagnostic yellow belly.

colonial_note.description: FIRST COLONIAL MAMMAL. Family groups of 10-20 individuals organized into a harem-polygynous system: one dominant male, 2-4 adult females (often related), yearlings, and juveniles. The colony shares burrow systems, operates a sentinel alarm system, and engages in social behaviors including greeting ceremonies, allogrooming, and play.
colonial_note.sentinel_system: One or more individuals perch on prominent boulders scanning for predators while others forage. When a sentinel detects a threat, it delivers the piercing whistle alarm. Colony members respond differently to aerial versus terrestrial predator alarms.
colonial_note.social_significance: The most complex mammalian social structure in the engine. Previous mammals were solitary-territorial (Pika), family-unit (Beaver), or loosely social (Raccoon). The marmot's colony is a true social group with hierarchies, cooperative behavior, and communal defense.

hibernation_note.description: HIBERNATION: 7-8 MONTHS (September/October through April/May). One of the longest hibernation periods in North American mammals. Body temperature drops from ~37 degrees C to as low as 5 degrees C. Heart rate slows from ~200 bpm to ~30 bpm. Metabolic rate decreases to ~5% of active levels. Periodic arousal bouts every 10-14 days.
hibernation_note.weight_loss: 40-50% of body weight lost during hibernation. A 5 kg marmot may emerge at 2.5-3 kg. Survives entirely on stored body fat.
hibernation_note.contrast_with_pika: The Pika (74) does NOT hibernate -- remains active year-round beneath snowpack, feeding on stored hay. The marmot does NOT store food -- fattens in late summer and survives on body fat. Same alpine challenge (7-8 months of winter), opposite strategies: hay storage (Pika) versus fat storage (marmot).

nodes.title: New Family: Sciuridae -- 35th Family
nodes.description: Sciuridae enters as the 35th family in the engine. The squirrel family. ~300 species in ~50 genera. Sixth new family in seven degrees. The engine's second Rodentia family (after Castoridae at 61).

nodes.title: Rodentia Returns: Second Rodent Family
nodes.description: Beaver (61, Castoridae) and Yellow-bellied Marmot (75, Sciuridae) represent two rodent families. Aquatic to alpine. Lodge to burrow. Nocturnal to diurnal. Rodentia spans river to summit.

nodes.title: Piercing Whistle Alarm -- 76th Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Sustained high-pitched whistle. 2,000-6,000 Hz. Audible at 300-500 meters. E=5. The loudest mammalian vocalization in the engine. Colony-wide alarm. Graded for predator type. The 'whistle pig.'

nodes.title: First Colonial Mammal -- Family Group Living
nodes.description: Family groups of 10-20 individuals. Harem-polygynous system. Sentinel behavior. Communal burrows. Social hierarchy. Greeting ceremonies, allogrooming, play. Most complex mammalian social structure in the engine.

nodes.title: Hibernation: 7-8 Months -- Fat Storage Strategy
nodes.description: Longest hibernation in the engine. Body temp to 5 degrees C. Heart rate to 30 bpm. 40-50% weight loss. Survives on stored body fat. Contrast with Pika: hay storage vs. fat storage. Same alpine challenge, opposite solutions.

nodes.title: Second Alpine Species (After Pika 74)
nodes.description: Rocky slopes at 1,200-3,500 meters. Second alpine species after Pika (74). Back-to-back alpine species. Overlapping habitat, different niches. The engine's alpine portrait deepens.

nodes.title: The Lonely Forest Parallel
nodes.description: Both loud and communal -- anthemic singalongs and colony-wide alarm choruses. Both the second act from the same place -- second Anacortes band (after Elverum 71) and second alpine species (after Pika 74). Both colonial/communal. Both high-energy (E=4 and E=5). The anthem and the whistle: communal sound.

nodes.title: American Pika Connection (SPS-074)
nodes.description: Alpine neighbor, different family. Both rocky-slope dwellers. Pika: tiny, solitary, does NOT hibernate, hay storage, E=4. Marmot: large, colonial, hibernates 7-8 months, fat storage, E=5. Back-to-back alpine species. Adjacent niches, opposite strategies.

nodes.title: American Beaver Connection (SPS-061)
nodes.description: Same order Rodentia, different families (Castoridae vs. Sciuridae). Aquatic vs. alpine. Lodge vs. burrow. Nocturnal vs. diurnal. Both large-bodied for their niches. Rodentia spans river to summit.

nodes.title: Red Fox Connection (SPS-071)
nodes.description: Predator-prey. The Red Fox is a significant marmot predator in alpine and subalpine meadows. The marmot's whistle alarm is frequently triggered by fox presence. The fox stalks; the marmot whistles; the colony dives.

nodes.title: Short-tailed Weasel Connection (SPS-067)
nodes.description: Predator-prey. The weasel can enter marmot burrows, particularly targeting juveniles. Adult marmots are too large for most weasels but young marmots are vulnerable.

nodes.title: American Marten Connection (SPS-065)
nodes.description: Predator-prey at the subalpine-alpine boundary. Both inhabit rocky terrain at elevation. The marten can take juvenile marmots. Both cold-adapted. Both dependent on specific rocky habitat structure.

nodes.title: Fourteenth Mammalian Entry -- Five Orders, Nine Families
nodes.description: Fourteen mammalian entries across fifteen degrees (61-75). Twelve species. Five orders. Nine families. Sciuridae is the 35th family, sixth new family in seven degrees.

cross_references.relationship: ALPINE NEIGHBOR, DIFFERENT FAMILY. Both rocky-slope dwellers. Pika: Ochotonidae (Lagomorpha), tiny (120-185 g), solitary-territorial, does NOT hibernate, hay storage, E=4, 'eenk' bark. Marmot: Sciuridae (Rodentia), large (1.6-5 kg), colonial, hibernates 7-8 months, fat storage, E=5, piercing whistle. Back-to-back alpine species. Same alpine challenge, opposite survival strategies.

SAME ORDER RODENTIA, DIFFERENT FAMILY (Castoridae vs. Sciuridae). Aquatic vs. alpine. Lodge vs. burrow. Nocturnal vs. diurnal. Both large-bodied for their niches. Rodentia now spans river to summit. Two rodent families, two opposite habitats.

PREDATOR-PREY. The Red Fox is a significant marmot predator in alpine and subalpine meadows. The marmot's whistle alarm is frequently triggered by fox presence. The fox stalks; the marmot whistles; the colony dives for burrows.
PREDATOR-PREY. The weasel's slender body can enter marmot burrows, particularly targeting juveniles. Adult marmots are too large for most weasels but young marmots are vulnerable to weasel predation.

PREDATOR-PREY AT SUBALPINE-ALPINE BOUNDARY. Both inhabit rocky terrain at elevation. The marten is a medium-sized predator that can take juvenile marmots. Both cold-adapted. Both dependent on specific rocky habitat structure.
Fourteenth mammalian entry. Twelfth species. Fifth order representation (Rodentia gains second family). Ninth family (Sciuridae). First colonial mammal.
Alpine/subalpine rocky slope habitat. Second alpine species. First colonial mammal. Hibernation 7-8 months. Sentinel alarm system. Fat storage survival strategy.
